variants.label: Australian Standard Patent Application
variants.description: Standard patent application under the Australian Patents Act 1990, including direct filings and national phase entries.

ui.placeholder: Enter a concise technical title of the invention.
ui.helpText: Short, specific technical title; do not include trademarks or the word 'patent'.

ui.placeholder: Identify related Australian, PCT or foreign applications, if any.
ui.helpText: Use for priority and divisional relationships; include application numbers and filing dates where relevant.

ui.placeholder: State the technical field or field of the invention.
ui.helpText: 1–3 sentences describing the technical field, consistent with IP Australia's specification guidance.

ui.placeholder: Describe relevant background art and problems.
ui.helpText: Explain prior techniques and issues they present without conceding obviousness.

ui.placeholder: Provide a high-level summary of the invention.
ui.helpText: Cover the technical problem, solution and advantageous effects of the invention in line with IP Australia guidance.

ui.placeholder: List each figure briefly, where drawings are provided.
ui.helpText: One line per figure, for example: 'Fig. 1 is a schematic view of a representative embodiment.'

ui.placeholder: Provide a full description of embodiments of the invention.
ui.helpText: Fully describe the invention so it can be performed, including the best method known, with examples and industrial applicability where relevant.
Draft clear, concise claims defining a single inventive concept; dependent claims may refer to one or more earlier claims.

ui.placeholder: Brief summary of the invention (50–150 words).
ui.helpText: Provide a concise technical summary; IP Australia guidance suggests 50–150 words.

sequenceListing.notes: If nucleotide and/or amino acid sequences are disclosed, a sequence listing compliant with WIPO ST.26 in XML format is required. IP Australia allows listings prepared using the WIPO Sequence Suite.

title.message: Title appears long; consider making it more concise.

abstract.message: Abstract exceeds the 150 word limit suggested by IP Australia.

claims.message: Number of claims exceeds 20; additional official fees may be payable in Australia.

crossSectionChecks.message: Some claim elements may not be clearly supported in the description of embodiments.
Abstract may describe features not clearly reflected in the independent claims.

title.instruction: Draft a concise technical title for the Australian standard patent application.
constraints: Do not exceed 500 characters.
Do not use trademarks or the word 'patent'.
Limit to 1–3 sentences.
State the technical field without describing advantages or embodiments.
Describe relevant prior art and its limitations.
Avoid explicit admissions that particular documents are prior art unless clearly signalled.
Do not state that the invention is obvious or trivial.

summary.instruction: Draft the Summary of the Invention section.
constraints: Outline the technical problem, the solution provided by the invention and its advantageous effects.
Ensure consistency with the independent claims.

detailed_description.instruction: Draft the Description of Embodiments section.
constraints: Describe embodiments in sufficient detail that a skilled person could perform the invention.
Disclose the best method known to the applicant of performing the invention.
Include examples and industrial applicability where appropriate.
Reference drawings and reference numerals where applicable.

claims.instruction: Draft a full set of claims compliant with Australian patent practice.
constraints: Use 'comprising' as the default open transitional term unless a closed scope is specifically required.
Draft each claim as a single sentence.
Dependent claims may refer to one or more previous claims, including other multiple dependent claims.
All claim features must be supported by the description of embodiments and define a single inventive concept.
Aim for 50–150 words; do not exceed 150 words.
Summarize the invention so that key technical features and use can be quickly understood.
Do not use claim-style language.

diagramGenerationHints.block: Use rectangles for components and arrows for data/control flow; keep labels consistent with the description of embodiments.
diagramGenerationHints.flowchart: Use standard flowchart symbols for processes and decisions; ensure the flow reflects the claimed method steps.
diagramGenerationHints.schematic: Show functional relationships and reference numerals clearly.
diagramGenerationHints.perspective_view: Use when overall physical form is important for understanding the invention.

checkList.description: Ensure all reference numerals in drawings are explained in the description of embodiments.
Ensure key claimed elements appear in at least one figure when drawings are present.
